summary: Armenian-American composer (1897–2000) known for his prolific output of 67 symphonies and deep affinity for mountains. His most famous symphony is No. 2, 'Mysterious Mountain.' He also wrote 'And God Created Great Whales,' which incorporates actual whale song recordings. His first symphony, Exile, commemorates the Armenian Genocide.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Alan Hovhaness (born Alan Vaness Chakmakjian, 1897–2000) was an Armenian-American composer known for writing 67 symphonies and a deep obsession with mountains. His father was from Armenia. He was commissioned by Leopold Stokowski (conductor of the Philadelphia Orchestra) for Mysterious Mountain. John Cage witnessed the debut of Hovhaness's Loudaszak, which may have inspired Cage's aleatoric style. Yolanda Kondonassis premiered his 'Spirit of Trees' for guitar and harp. He also wrote 'Garden of Adonis' for harp and flute.

examples: For 10 points, name this set of 67 orchestral works by a mountain-obsessed 20th-century Armenian-American composer.
This Armenian American composer wrote the symphonies Mount St. Helens, To the Appalachian Mountains, and Mysterious Mountain.

works.name: Symphony No. 2 ('Mysterious Mountain')

works.name: Other Symphonies
works.description: Hovhaness wrote 67 symphonies in total. His Symphony No. 1 ('Exile') commemorates the Armenian Genocide with movements titled 'Lament,' 'Conflict,' and 'Triumph.' Symphony No. 21 was dedicated to Vasken, the Catholicos of Etchmiadzin, and features the city's bells. Symphony No. 22 ('City of Light'), commissioned by the Birmingham Symphony (whose conductor's father-in-law conducted that orchestra), opens with five D major chords with a dissonant vibraphone F natural on the third. Symphony No. 50 ('Mount St. Helens') depicts a volcanic eruption; a solo flute bridges the opening 'dawn theme' and the eruption in the final movement, and the second movement is nicknamed 'Spirit Lake.' Symphony No. 66 is 'Hymn to Glacial Peak.' He also wrote 'To the Appalachian Mountains.'

examples: 'Lament,' 'Conflict,' and 'Triumph' are the three movements of the first of these pieces, which memorializes a genocide.
This composer's first symphony, Exile, commemorates the Armenian Genocide.

works.name: And God Created Great Whales
works.description: A symphonic poem by Hovhaness that juxtaposes actual recordings of whale songs with melodies drawn from a major pentatonic scale. Along with John Tavener's The Whale, it is one of the earliest works to promote saving whales. The title comes from the Book of Genesis. The piece includes descending glissandi marked 'wild and powerful' from three trombones. Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
Alan Hovhaness (born Alan Vaness Chakmakjian, 1897–2000) was an Armenian-American composer celebrated for his prolific output — most notably a set of 67 symphonies — and his obsession with mountains. His father was from Armenia. He is primarily associated with Armenian-American musical identity alongside Aram Khachaturian.

Hovhaness's best-known work is Symphony No. 2, nicknamed 'Mysterious Mountain,' which was commissioned by and premiered under Leopold Stokowski of the Philadelphia Orchestra. His first symphony, 'Exile,' commemorates the Armenian Genocide and has three movements titled 'Lament,' 'Conflict,' and 'Triumph.' Symphony No. 21 was dedicated to Vasken, the Catholicos of Etchmiadzin (the seat of the Armenian Apostolic Church), and its three movements feature the city's bells of war. Symphony No. 22, 'City of Light' (depicting 'a million lights'), was commissioned by the Birmingham Symphony and opens with five D major chords while the vibraphone plays a dissonant F natural on the third chord. Symphony No. 50, 'Mount St. Helens,' depicts a volcanic eruption: a solo flute bridges the opening 'dawn theme' and the eruption in the final movement, and the second movement is nicknamed 'Spirit Lake.' Symphony No. 66 is titled 'Hymn to Glacial Peak.' Other symphonic titles include 'To the Appalachian Mountains.'

Hovhaness also wrote the symphonic poem And God Created Great Whales (its title from the Book of Genesis), which juxtaposes actual recordings of whale songs with melodies drawn from a major pentatonic scale. Along with John Tavener's The Whale, it is considered one of the earliest works to promote saving whales. John Cage witnessed the debut of Hovhaness's Loudaszak, which may have influenced Cage's development of aleatory music. Yolanda Kondonassis premiered Hovhaness's 'Spirit of Trees' for guitar and harp; he also wrote 'Garden of Adonis' for harp and flute.

Composer: John Cage witnessed the debut of his piece Loudaszak, which may have inspired Cage's aleatoric style
Symphony: No. 1 ('Exile') commemorates the Armenian Genocide; three movements titled 'Lament,' 'Conflict,' and 'Triumph'
Symphony: No. 21, dedicated to the Catholicos of Etchmiadzin (Vasken); features that city's bells of war across three movements
Symphony: No. 22 ('City of Light'), commissioned by the Birmingham Symphony; opens with five D major chords, but the vibraphone plays a dissonant F natural on the third chord
Symphony: No. 50 ('Mount St. Helens'); solo flute bridges the 'dawn theme' and the eruption in the final movement; second movement nicknamed 'Spirit Lake'
Tone Poem: incorporates actual whale sound recordings juxtaposed with major pentatonic melodies; title from the Book of Genesis; one of the earliest works to promote saving whales
cards.back: And God Created Great Whales (Alan Hovhaness)
